claim: The Fe–8% Al alloy was rolled in three successive passes with cumulative strain ε=1, resulting in a fully recrystallized microstructure with ~800 μm equiaxed grains and isotropic morphology.
evidence_text: The investigated ferritic Fe–8% Al alloy was supplied by ArcelorMittal Maizieres and rolled in three successive passes. The cumulative deformation is equal to 1. This steel is 100% recrystallized with an initial grain size of ~800 lm (equiaxed grains). The material is morphologically isotropic.

claim: Hot torsion tests were performed under argon atmosphere at 900–1100 °C and strain rates 0.1–10 s⁻¹ with negative shear; samples were quenched with argon within 0.5 s at a cooling rate of ~200 °C/s.
evidence_text: hot torsion tests were performed in a direction involving a negative shear and under argon atmosphere to reduce the oxidation phenomena, within the temperature range 900–1100 -C and strain rate range 0.1–10 s-1. After deformation, samples were quenched immediately (0.5 s) with argon in order to retain the microstructures and avoid post-dynamic recrystallization. The cooling rate is ~200 °C/s during the first seconds.

claim: EBSD analyses were performed near the surface at 0.9 radius on the θz plane, with grain boundaries (θ ≥ 15°) displayed as black lines and subgrain boundaries (2° ≤ θ < 15°) as red lines.
evidence_text: For all test conditions, the analyses were performed near the surface (0.9 radius). The observation plane is hz and the z-axis is parallel to the vertical direction in the electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) maps. Black lines and red lines are used for grain boundaries (misorientations θ ≥ 15°) and subgrain boundaries (2° ≤ θ < 15°) respectively.

claim: Under torsion at 1000 °C, 0.1 s⁻¹ and negative shear, the strongest texture component is D2: {1-12}<111> (self-symmetric), with maximum intensity at ε=15.
evidence_text: In the case of negative shear and at large strain, the strongest component is D2: -1-12<111> which is “selfsymmetric”, i.e. made of one single ideal orientation that fulfils the symmetry conditions by itself. The maximal intensity of the D2 component is observed for e = 15.

claim: At 1100 °C, ε=2, and strain rates of 5 s⁻¹ and 10 s⁻¹, the microstructure is a mixture of small equiaxed recrystallized grains free of substructure and large elongated original grains with internal misorientations, indicating discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX).
evidence_text: The microstructure appears as a mixture of “young” grains containing no substructure, and “old” grains displaying internal misorientations. In these conditions, instead of CDRX a mechanism of discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX) occurs.

claim: The misorientation distributions for DDRX conditions (1100 °C, 5 and 10 s⁻¹) are close to the MacKenzie distribution for randomly oriented cubic crystals, with few LABs and a maximum number of HABs near 45°.
evidence_text: With this kind of mechanism of recrystallization, the distributions of misorientations (Fig. 5) are totally different compared to the previous case (Fig. 2). Indeed, the distribution is close to that derived by MacKenzie for cubic crystals uniformly distributed in the orientation space (set of randomly oriented crystals) with few LABs and a maximum number of HABs close to 45°.

claim: The Fe–8% Al ferritic alloy has a density of ~7 g cm⁻³, providing a weight reduction of about 10% relative to conventional steels.
evidence_text: Ferritic Fe–8% Al alloys have a density of ~7 g cm-3 , which allows a reduction in density of ~10% compared to conventional steels [2].
